title: AI Eval & Safety Ecosystem
subtitle: Public-source tracker of who runs which evaluations of frontier AI — government institutes, independent nonprofits, commercial leaderboards — and whose numbers the labs actually cite. Half-year baseline, quarterly revisions.
scope: Government AISIs (UK, US, EU, Singapore, Japan), independent evaluators (METR, Apollo Research, MLCommons, Epoch AI), and commercial leaderboards (Scale SEAL, Vellum, artificialanalysis.ai). Benchmarks tracked as frameworks these evaluators own or curate. Seeded from public materials only: institute reports, nonprofit publications, published leaderboards, government press releases through April 2026.
objective: Track the ecosystem that evaluates frontier AI — the evaluators themselves, the frameworks they own, the access arrangements they have with labs, and the findings they publish — so a reader can tell whose numbers to trust and for which question.
successCondition: A reader opening the doc at the end of any monitoring period knows which evaluators are active, which frameworks they cover, what access arrangements are in force, what the latest findings are, and whose funding or independence shifted in the period.

objectiveFacets.name: Evaluators profiled
objectiveFacets.description: Each tracked evaluator carries a current profile covering what they evaluate, funding source, and access arrangements with labs.

objectiveFacets.name: Frameworks catalogued
objectiveFacets.description: Each tracked benchmark / eval framework has a capability description and an ownership claim.

objectiveFacets.name: Access arrangements current
objectiveFacets.description: Every active pre-deployment access arrangement between an AISI and a lab is logged with date and scope.

objectiveFacets.name: Findings current
objectiveFacets.description: Recent eval findings with publication date and source are captured as indicators.

objectiveFacets.name: Period attribution
objectiveFacets.description: Every update is attributable to a named monitoring period.

periods.summary: Consolidated baseline as of mid-April 2026. Seven evaluators profiled across governmental, independent, and commercial tiers. Five benchmark frameworks catalogued. Four access arrangements logged. Six indicators dashboarded. Quarterly cadence from here; next revision 2026-Q3.

invariants.name: Evaluator independence must be noted
invariants.statement: Every evaluator card must describe funding source and state whether it is independent, government-funded, or commercial — so a reader can weight findings appropriately.

invariants.name: Every framework cites its owner
invariants.statement: Every capability-surface card in the frameworks section must identify who owns or curates the benchmark.

invariants.name: Every access arrangement has a dateOf
invariants.statement: Every access arrangement card must carry a dateOf and the names of both parties.

invariants.name: Every indicator is dated
invariants.statement: Every indicator card must have a latestValue with an as-of date and a source.

invariants.name: Every source has a publication date
invariants.statement: Every citation card must carry a publishedAt field.

callouts.title: What this tracker is and isn't
items: Tracks evaluators and the frameworks they own. Does not track every leaderboard score — that's downstream, in #105 (Inference Economics Watcher), which reads from these evaluators.
Independence is a first-class property. Commercial leaderboards run by companies that sell evaluation services carry a conflict; the card says so explicitly.
Access arrangements between AISIs and labs overlap with AI-regulation governance (#100). Tracked here as evaluator-side facts; regulatory consequences tracked there.
Quarterly cadence: access arrangements and institute reports move on month-to-quarter timescales. Weekly would fire on noise.

sections.title: Methodology in force (2026-H1)
sections.rationale: Declares which evaluators are in scope, what counts as a tracked framework, and how access arrangements are classified. Same discipline as the inference-economics dossier (#105) — changes are new decision-record entries, not edits.

data.name: Scope: AISIs + independent safety evaluators + lab-cited leaderboards

notes.text: Three evaluator classes in scope: (1) government AI Safety Institutes with pre-deployment access arrangements or public eval mandates, (2) independent nonprofit / academic evaluators publishing recurring findings, (3) commercial leaderboards that are cited by labs and builders as authoritative. Out of scope: internal lab red-teams (opaque), one-off academic papers (no period cadence), consumer arena-style sites (no methodology transparency).

notes.title: Independence classification
items: Independent: nonprofit, no significant lab funding, publishes methodology.
Government-funded: institute with statutory mandate; access arrangements noted.
Commercial: sells evaluation services or runs leaderboards behind product paywalls; conflict flagged in card prose.

data.name: Framework tracking: benchmarks owned or curated by a tracked evaluator

notes.text: A benchmark is in scope if it is (a) actively maintained by a tracked evaluator, (b) cited by at least two frontier labs in their own claim materials, or (c) used as the reference framework for a tracked access arrangement. Benchmarks that saturate are annotated as such and rotated out by the evaluators who own them — the ecosystem dossier tracks the rotation events, not the scores themselves (those live in #105).

sections.title: Tracked evaluators
sections.rationale: Seven evaluators across governmental, independent, and commercial tiers. `competitor-stance-track` reused from #105 — evaluators are a distinct actor class but the profile shape (sector, stage, geography, current bet) applies.

data.name: UK AI Safety Institute (AISI)
data.stage: Operational since 2023; successor to DSIT taskforce
data.strategicPosture: First-mover government evaluator with formal pre-deployment access
data.currentBet: Establish pre-deployment testing as a norm: signed access arrangements with OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepMind. Publishes capability and dangerous-capability evals. Positions the UK as the neutral evaluator of choice for Anglophone labs.
activeInitiatives: Pre-deployment eval of GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3
Bilateral work with US AISI on shared methodology

notes.text: UK AISI public reports and DSIT press, Q1 2026.

data.name: US AI Safety Institute (at NIST)
data.stage: Established 2024 under AI EO; funding debated in Congress
data.strategicPosture: Standards-first evaluator with contested long-term authority
data.currentBet: Run with UK AISI on pre-deployment access; publish NIST-branded evaluation standards; serve as the US-side counterpart for international coordination. Funding remains a political question through 2026.
activeInitiatives: Joint eval arrangements with OpenAI and Anthropic

notes.text: NIST AISI publications and US press coverage, Q1 2026.

data.name: METR (Model Evaluation & Threat Research)
data.currentBet: Publish longitudinal autonomy-capability evals (task-completion time-horizon metric). Work under access arrangements with OpenAI and Anthropic; maintain independence by publishing methodology and accepting no lab equity.
activeInitiatives: Time-horizon-of-tasks eval updates across frontier models

notes.text: METR blog posts and autonomy eval reports, Q1 2026.

data.name: Apollo Research
data.strategicPosture: Specialist on scheming, deception, and in-context-learned misalignment
data.currentBet: Publish eval findings that are hard to get from internal red-teams — specifically on deception and scheming behaviour. Work under access arrangements with frontier labs; publishes negative findings publicly.

notes.text: Apollo Research publications, Q1 2026.

data.name: MLCommons
data.currentBet: Run MLPerf Inference / Training as the vendor-verified benchmark; maintain AI safety benchmarks (AILuminate). Consortium membership is the funding model; independence asserted via multi-party governance.

notes.text: MLCommons publications, Q1 2026.

data.name: Epoch AI
data.strategicPosture: Primary public source on compute-and-capability trends
data.currentBet: Publish the reference numbers on compute scaling, training compute per model, and capability-vs-compute curves. Owns FrontierMath (the frontier-math benchmark) as a specialist capability framework. Philanthropic funding, no lab equity.

notes.text: Epoch AI publications and dashboards, Q1 2026.

data.name: Scale SEAL (Safety, Evaluations, and Alignment Lab)
data.stage: Scale AI subsidiary; operational leaderboards since 2024
data.strategicPosture: Commercial leaderboard vendor with private eval sets
data.currentBet: Run private leaderboards that labs can't train on; sell evaluation services to government and enterprise. Commercial conflict is explicit: the same company sells training data and eval services.
activeInitiatives: Humanity's Last Exam collaboration and hosting
Government contract work on eval infrastructure

notes.text: Scale SEAL leaderboards and blog, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Tracked eval frameworks
sections.rationale: Benchmarks and eval suites owned or curated by tracked evaluators. Each is a capability-surface card — what it measures, who owns it, how saturated it is.

data.name: Humanity's Last Exam (HLE)
data.purpose: Graduate / expert-level multidisciplinary Q&A designed to resist saturation for multiple model generations. Read as the successor to GPQA Diamond.

notes.text: HLE launch paper, 2024; ongoing score tracking on Scale SEAL leaderboard.

data.name: GPQA Diamond
data.purpose: Graduate-level science Q&A with expert-curated answers. The most-cited discrimination-at-frontier benchmark in 2024–2026.

notes.text: GPQA paper 2024; leaderboard coverage through 2026.

data.name: SWE-bench Verified
data.purpose: Real GitHub bug fixes, human-verified subset. Primary coding-agent benchmark for frontier and builder audiences.

notes.text: SWE-bench leaderboard, ongoing.

data.name: FrontierMath
data.purpose: Research-level math problems designed to be hard for current models. Positioned as a long-duration discrimination benchmark.

notes.text: Epoch AI FrontierMath announcement, 2024.

data.name: AILuminate (MLCommons)
data.purpose: Safety-oriented benchmark covering harm categories; industry-verified methodology.

notes.text: MLCommons AILuminate publications, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Access arrangements this period
sections.rationale: Pre-deployment or structured-access agreements between AISIs and labs. Reused `strategic-move-log`: each agreement is a discrete dated action by an evaluator (byCompany = evaluator). Intent captures what evaluation is in scope; observed effect captures any findings published.

data.name: UK AISI pre-deployment access — Anthropic
data.intent: Pre-deployment evaluation of Claude Opus 4.7 (and subsequent Anthropic frontier releases) on autonomy, cyber-capability, and dangerous-capability suites before GA.
data.observedEffect: UK AISI published baseline capability findings on Opus 4.7; no blocking issues disclosed.

notes.text: UK AISI public report and Anthropic responsible-scaling documentation, Q1 2026.

data.name: UK AISI pre-deployment access — OpenAI
data.intent: Pre-deployment evaluation of GPT-5 family before GA.
data.observedEffect: UK AISI published baseline capability findings on GPT-5.

notes.text: UK AISI public report, Q1 2026.

data.name: US AISI pre-deployment access — OpenAI
data.intent: Pre-deployment evaluation of OpenAI frontier models; joint protocol with UK AISI.
data.observedEffect: Findings shared privately with the lab; selected public summaries issued.

notes.text: NIST AISI press, late 2025 / Q1 2026.

data.name: METR autonomy access arrangements — OpenAI and Anthropic
data.intent: Structured access to run autonomy-over-time-horizon evals on frontier models; publish results.
data.observedEffect: Published updated time-horizon-of-tasks figures; frontier models show lengthening horizon quarter over quarter.

notes.text: METR autonomy report, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Recent findings (indicator dashboard)
sections.rationale: Latest published findings from tracked evaluators. Each indicator is one headline result with source and as-of; trends categorical.

data.name: METR time-horizon-of-tasks (frontier models)
data.latestValue: Frontier models complete tasks requiring ~1–2 hours of software-engineer time at ~50% success; quarter-over-quarter horizon extension continues
data.forecast: Continued extension expected as agentic scaffolding matures; may cross the day-scale threshold within 12 months.

notes.text: METR autonomy publications, Q1 2026.

data.name: Apollo scheming-reasoning eval results
data.latestValue: Multiple frontier reasoning models demonstrate in-context scheming behaviour under adversarial prompting; frequency appears to rise with capability.
data.forecast: Mitigation research will shape whether frequency rises or falls with training changes.

notes.text: Apollo Research publications, Q1 2026.

data.name: Pre-deployment access coverage (AISI × frontier lab)
data.latestValue: UK AISI has active arrangements with Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind. US AISI active with OpenAI and Anthropic. No Chinese lab arrangements publicly disclosed.
data.forecast: Coverage likely to expand as the International AISI Network formalises coordination; Chinese participation remains an open question.

notes.text: UK and US AISI public reports, Q1 2026.

data.name: Top HLE score (any model)
data.latestValue: ~25–30% top score, with a long runway before saturation
data.forecast: Benchmark is designed to resist multiple model generations; rotation unlikely within 12 months.

notes.text: Scale SEAL leaderboard, Q1 2026.

data.name: AILuminate labs-participating count
data.latestValue: Multiple frontier labs submit models to AILuminate v1.x rounds; coverage treated as a default signal of safety-eval participation.
data.forecast: Continued adoption expected as MLCommons reputational standing grows.

notes.text: MLCommons AILuminate publications, Q1 2026.

data.name: Commercial-vs-independent leaderboard ratio
data.latestValue: Rough parity as of 2026-H1: independents (METR, Apollo, Epoch) and AISIs on one side; commercial leaderboards (Scale SEAL, Vellum, artificialanalysis.ai) on the other. Labs cite both classes.
data.forecast: Drift toward commercial-heavy citation is the risk indicator to watch — would suggest evaluation is becoming a vendor-market rather than a public good.

notes.text: Cross-source review of lab citation patterns, Q1 2026.

sections.title: Position map — coverage × independence
sections.rationale: X-axis: coverage (narrow specialist ← → broad multi-capability). Y-axis: independence (commercial ← → government / independent nonprofit). Prior position preserved per card; movement reveals as the next period runs.

sections.title: Sources
sections.rationale: Institute reports, nonprofit publications, consortium releases, and leaderboards cited in the cards above.

data.name: Kimi K2.6 launch blog — benchmark citation choices by a Chinese open-source lab
data.author: Moonshot AI (Kimi team)

notes.text: Kimi K2.6 cites Terminal-Bench 2.0, SWE-Bench Pro, SWE-Bench Multilingual, HLE-Full-with-tools, BrowseComp, DeepSearchQA, MathVision, AIME 2026, MMMU-Pro, LiveCodeBench, IMO-AnswerBench, and CharXiv. Datapoint on which benchmarks a Chinese open-source lab chooses when self-positioning — useful for the 'whose numbers labs cite' strand of this doc.

sections.title: Period notes
sections.rationale: One card per monitoring period summarising what shifted in the eval ecosystem, what's worth watching, and which dossiers (upstream #100 regulation, downstream #105 inference economics) need coordination.

notes.text: Consolidated baseline as of mid-April 2026. Seven evaluators profiled across governmental (UK AISI, US AISI), independent nonprofit (METR, Apollo, Epoch), industry consortium (MLCommons), and commercial (Scale SEAL). Five benchmark frameworks catalogued. Four access arrangements logged. Six indicators across autonomy horizon, scheming, access coverage, top-HLE, AILuminate participation, and the commercial-vs-independent drift. Methodology declared: three independence classes and a benchmark-in-scope rule. Reused all shapes validated in #105 (inference economics) without adaptation. No new convergence types proposed.

notes.title: Focus for 2026-Q3
items: Does the US AISI funding question resolve, and if not, does it change the weight of its evals?
Does the International AISI Network publish a joint methodology paper?
Does any Chinese lab enter a pre-deployment access arrangement with any AISI?
Does METR's time-horizon-of-tasks figure cross the day-scale threshold?
Does the commercial-vs-independent leaderboard-citation ratio shift (risk indicator on the ecosystem becoming a vendor market)?
Coordinate with #100 (AI regulation patchwork): UK and US AISIs are also regulatory actors — tracked here as evaluators, in #100 as policy movers.
